input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The serial robot MABI MAX 100, specifically its first axis, comprising a permanent magnet synchronous motor, gear transmission, and cycloidal gear. Operational domain is industrial robotics, with key characteristics including precise motion control requirements and real-time monitoring of mechanical components for digital twinning suitability.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Services include friction modeling for motion accuracy optimization, real-time monitoring of mechanical component behavior, and visualization via a unified dashboard for operators and engineers to assess system performance and control.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation utilizes Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies including SysML for system modeling, RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for handling complexity across abstraction levels. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs) describe components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavior modeling is supported by MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines, with simulation and detailed behavior handled via MathWorks Simulink, other simulation tools, and general-purpose programming languages like C++ and Java.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin integrates Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) using SysML for structural modeling across requirements, functional, logical, and technical abstraction levels, complemented by Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs for component & connector perspectives. Behavioral aspects are modeled via MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines, with detailed simulations executed in MathWorks Simulink and code implementations in C++ or Java. Data management handles heterogeneous model sources and integrates stakeholder perspectives through multi-tool workflows.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Physical actuators include permanent magnet synchronous motors for each axis, coupled with gear transmissions and cycloidal gears for mechanical reduction. Control is managed by a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller, which processes commands and adjusts actuator movements through precise servo control loops.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure comprises two position encoders per drive: a motor encoder mounted directly on the motor and a secondary encoder positioned behind the gearbox. Additionally, input current data is collected from the motors for performance monitoring, with measurements transmitted in real-time for system analysis.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is validated by comparing simulated data against measured physical system data, where deviation serves as the primary validation metric. The system addresses inaccuracies in robot motion through continuous calibration, as specified in the solution scope to improve overall motion accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time series sensor data (input current and output angle) are streamed from physical components to the digital shadow using the MQTT protocol.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol is used for sensor data transmission; no details on network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, or security protocols are specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process involves four main steps: Problem identification to define objectives, Asset and model analysis to assess physical assets and existing models, Use or creation of calculations or models for simulation, and Data identification to determine required data streams. These steps are part of the methodology for deriving concrete digital shadows as described in Sections II.C and IV.A of the source document.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The system generates insights through simulation models to enhance understanding of joint friction and improve robot motion accuracy. Analytics are processed via a dedicated Processing component, with user interaction facilitated through integrated visualization within a unified dashboard.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with industry standards including SysML [17] for systems engineering modeling, ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25,26] for architecture description languages, and SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs) [17,27], facilitating interoperability among heterogeneous stakeholders and systems [31].

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly specific and accurate to the source text. It correctly identifies the system as the 'serial robot MABI MAX 100' and narrows the scope to the 'first axis'. It accurately lists the components: 'permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'gear transmission', and 'cycloidal gear'. The operational domain and characteristics regarding motion control and monitoring are also supported by the text. Quotes: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100', 'The first vertical robot axis at the base is used for creating a digital shadow', 'first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'followed by a cycloidal gear', 'improve the accuracy of the robot'.

The extraction accurately identifies the core services: friction modeling for accuracy, real-time monitoring of behavior, and visualization via a dashboard. These are explicitly stated in the text. However, the specific mention of 'operators and engineers' is a minor inference (the text mentions 'stakeholders'), and describing 'control' as a direct service provided by the DT is slightly interpretative, as the text states the DT enhances accuracy to support control rather than executing it directly. Quotes: 'combine white- and black-box friction models... ultimately improve robot motion accuracy', 'digital shadows — real-time, data-driven representations of robot joint behavior', 'live-data linking as well as visualization in a dashboard'.

The extraction is highly detailed and directly supported by Section II.A. It accurately lists MBSE methodologies: 'RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT', which the text describes as 'different methodologies on how to handle this complexity'. It correctly identifies ADLs: 'ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)', which are cited as describing 'component & connector perspective'. It mentions behavior modeling with 'MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines', matching the text 'e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in SysML v1.7'. Finally, it lists 'MathWorks Simulink', 'C++', and 'Java' for simulation and coding, supported by the text 'MathWorks Simulink... or code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java'.

The extraction accurately describes the four-step methodology for deriving digital shadows: Problem identification, Asset and model analysis, Use or creation of calculations or models, and Data identification. These steps are explicitly detailed in Section II.C and applied in Section IV.A. The extraction is specific to the methodology described in the text. Quotes: '1) Problem identification: To get all relevant requirements...', '2) Asset and model analysis: One has to choose the parts of the asset...', '3) Use or creation of calculations or models...', '4) Data identification: We have to identify relevant data traces...'.

The extraction correctly identifies that the system generates insights through simulation models to enhance understanding of joint friction and improve robot motion accuracy. However, the extraction could be more detailed regarding the specific use of white-box and black-box models mentioned in the text.

The extraction is highly specific and accurate, directly referencing the standards listed in Section II.A. It correctly identifies 'SysML [17]' for systems engineering modeling and 'ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML IBDs [17], [27]' as architecture description languages used to model the system. The extraction accurately reflects the text's discussion on integrating heterogeneous stakeholders and systems.
